channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: An asteroid discovered days ago will narrowly miss Earth - CNN
item.description:
1. An asteroid discovered days ago will narrowly miss Earth  CNN
  2. Newly discovered asteroid 2026 JH2 will make a close, but safe, encounter with Earth on Monday, NASA says  ABC7 Los Angeles
  3. Scientists say a newly discovered, blue-whale-size asteroid will fly super close to Earth on Monday  Live Science
  4. Near-Earth Object 2026 JH2 Ranks Zero on the Loeb Scale  Avi Loeb – Medium
  5. Watch an asteroid the size of a blue whale hurtle towards Earth live online TODAY  Space

item.description:
1. SpaceX Dragon capsule delivers science and supplies to space station  Space

  3. SpaceX launches CRS-34 cargo mission to ISS  SpaceNews
  4. NASA, SpaceX launch Dragon mission with 6,500 pounds of science and supplies to the space station  Spaceflight Now
  5. SpaceX launches Dragon cargo ship on unpiloted flight to space station  CBS News

item.title: The James Webb Space Telescope is currently observing galaxies that formed 13 billion years ago, and several of them shouldn't exist according to the model cosmologists were using when Webb launched - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The James Webb Space Telescope is currently observing galaxies that formed 13 billion years ago, and several of them shouldn't exist according to the model cosmologists were using when Webb launched  Space Daily
  2. Supernova dust may be behind one of JWST's biggest puzzles  Phys.org
  3. Webb Telescope Reveals Brown Dwarfs Masquerading as Early Galaxies  Sky & Telescope
  4. Astronomers Stunned by Ancient Galaxy With No Spin  SciTechDaily
  5. Massive black hole from the early universe has astronomers looking back to the Big Bang  AOL.com

item.title: The US space enterprise is desperately waiting for Starship—will it finally deliver? - Ars Technica
item.description:
1. The US space enterprise is desperately waiting for Starship—will it finally deliver?  Ars Technica

  4. SpaceX reschedules 12th Starship launch  ValleyCentral.com

item.description:

  4. A new species offers a clue to the boom of giant dinosaurs  National Geographic

item.title: NASA releases final RFP for Mars communications orbiter - SpaceNews
item.description:
1. NASA releases final RFP for Mars communications orbiter  SpaceNews
  2. NASA Draws on Industry for Mars Telecommunications Network  NASA (.gov)
  3. NASA just put a 30-day clock on a $700 million Mars contract, and the deadline tells you everything about how scared the agency is of losing its relay orbiters before astronauts arrive  Space Daily
  4. What’s up with… NASA, telco M&A, Verizon  telecomtv.com
  5. NASA Kicks Off Mars Telecom Network Competition  Aviation Week

item.title: SpaceX Starlink and other satellite megaconstellations are creating an 'unregulated geoengineering experiment', scientists say - Space
item.description:
1. SpaceX Starlink and other satellite megaconstellations are creating an 'unregulated geoengineering experiment', scientists say  Space

  4. A Falcon 9 upper stage burned up over Europe last February, and German scientists just caught it doing something to the atmosphere nobody had directly measured before  Space Daily

item.title: The four astronauts who flew farther from Earth than any humans in history have just come back. The advice they're giving the rest of us has almost nothing to do with space. - Space Daily
item.description:

item.title: The ISS travels at 17,500 miles per hour, which means astronauts inside it are aging measurably slower than people on the ground, and by the time Scott Kelly returned from his year in orbit he was 5 milliseconds younger than his identical twin brother Mark. - Space Daily
item.description: The ISS travels at 17,500 miles per hour, which means astronauts inside it are aging measurably slower than people on the ground, and by the time Scott Kelly returned from his year in orbit he was 5 milliseconds younger than his identical twin brother Mark.  Space Daily

item.title: The Parker Solar Probe is now moving at 430,000 miles per hour, fast enough to cross the continental United States in 20 seconds, and it survives skimming the Sun's corona because a four-and-a-half-inch slab of carbon foam stays at room temperature on the - Space Daily
